title: Collection-specific Exists method should be preferred instead of the Any extension
description: Both the List.Exists method and IEnumerable.Any method can be used to find the first element that satisfies a predicate in a collection. However, List.Exists can be faster than IEnumerable.Any for List objects, as well as requires significantly less memory. For small collections, the performance difference may be negligible, but for large collections, it can be noticeable.
recommendation: Replace with the recommended alternative approach.

noncompliant: bool hasMatch = list.Any(x => x > 0); // Noncompliant: using LINQ extension method
compliant: bool hasMatch = list.Exists(x => x > 0); // Compliant: using collection-specific method

logic.reasoning: Detects when .Any() LINQ extension method is used on a List<T> variable instead of the more efficient List.Exists() method. Tracks List<T> variable declarations (var x = new List<> or List<T> x = new List<>) and checks for .Any() calls on those specific variables. Excludes: (1) IEnumerable variables - rule only applies to List, (2) Arrays - don't have Exists method, (3) Logging/formatting - display contexts are OK. The performance difference matters for large collections.

should_trigger: var list = new List<int>(); bool hasMatch = list.Any(x => x > 0);
List<string> names = new List<string>(); bool hasLong = names.Any(n => n.Length > 10);
should_not_trigger: var list = new List<int>(); bool hasMatch = list.Exists(x => x > 0);
IEnumerable<int> enumerable = GetNumbers(); bool anyPositive = enumerable.Any(x => x > 0);
int[] array = new int[] { 1, 2, 3 }; bool arrayHasMatch = array.Any(x => x > 0);
